children.name: Lanius
children.description: The typical shrikes — passerine birds of open country with a hooked bill and a raptor-like hunting style. Shrikes earned the name 'butcher birds' from their habit of impaling prey on thorns and barbed wire as a larder. Despite being songbirds, they hunt insects, small mammals, lizards, and even other birds, striking from exposed perches with surgical precision. The genus is widely distributed across Eurasia, Africa, and North America.

children.name: Lanius excubitor
children.description: The largest shrike in Europe. Pale grey upperparts, black mask, white underparts, and bold white wing patches. Resident in Sweden year-round, where it inhabits open farmland and clear-cuts with scattered perches. Hunts from exposed treetops and utility wires, impaling surplus prey on thorns. Its song is a surprisingly melodic series of whistles and trills.

children.name: Lanius borealis
children.description: The New World counterpart of the Great Grey Shrike, recently split as a distinct species. Breeds across the taiga of Alaska, Canada, and Siberia. Winters in southern Canada and the northern United States. Slightly darker above and more heavily barred below than the Great Grey Shrike.

children.name: Lanius meridionalis
children.description: Formerly considered conspecific with the Great Grey Shrike. Ranges from the Iberian Peninsula and North Africa through the Middle East and Central Asia to northern India. Inhabits arid and semi-arid open landscapes with scattered scrub. Has a stronger bill and paler plumage than the northern species.

children.name: Lanius ludovicianus
children.description: North America's only endemic shrike. Smaller and darker than the Northern Shrike, with a more extensive black mask. Inhabits open farmland and grassland with scattered shrubs. Populations have declined sharply across much of its range due to habitat loss and pesticide use. Classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Lanius collurio
children.description: A striking summer visitor to Sweden and much of Europe. The male has a chestnut-red back, grey crown, black mask, and pink-tinged underparts. Females and juveniles are warmer brown with fine barring. Winters in southern Africa. Once common in Swedish farmland, it has declined due to agricultural intensification. Its call is a harsh 'chack-chack'.

children.name: Lanius phoenicuroides
children.description: Breeding from Central Asia to Iran and wintering in East Africa. Formerly considered a subspecies of the Red-backed Shrike. Distinguished by its rufous crown and nape and a white supercilium. Vagrant individuals occasionally reach Europe.

children.name: Lanius isabellinus
children.description: Breeds in Central Asia and winters in East Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Sandy-brown upperparts with a pale supercilium and a black mask. A rare but regular vagrant to western Europe in autumn.

children.name: Lanius minor
children.description: Slightly smaller than the Great Grey Shrike, with a more extensive black mask reaching across the forehead. Grey upperparts, white underparts, and a pink flush on the breast. Breeds in southeastern Europe and Central Asia; winters in southern Africa. A rare visitor to Sweden.

children.name: Lanius senator
children.description: A colourful southern European shrike with a chestnut crown, black mask, white forehead, and pure white underparts. Breeds around the Mediterranean, wintering in the Sahel zone of Africa. A rare vagrant to northern Europe, including Sweden. Favours open woodland and orchards.

children.name: Lanius nubicus
children.description: The smallest European shrike. Strikingly patterned with a black mask extending to the nape, white forehead, orange flanks, and white wing patches. Breeds in the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East, wintering in northeastern Africa. The most arboreal of the European shrikes.

children.name: Lanius cristatus
children.description: A widespread Asian shrike breeding from Siberia to Japan and wintering in South and Southeast Asia. Warm brown upperparts, buff underparts, and a prominent black mask. A rare vagrant to Europe and North America. Inhabits open woodland, forest edges, and cultivated areas.

children.name: Lanius tigrinus
children.description: A small, striking shrike from East Asia. The male has a grey crown, chestnut back with bold black barring (hence 'tiger'), and white underparts. Breeds in northeastern China, Korea, and Japan; winters in Southeast Asia. Favours dense shrubbery and forest edges.

children.name: Lanius schach
children.description: A conspicuous and widespread shrike across South, East, and Southeast Asia. Grey crown, black mask, chestnut back, and a notably long, graduated tail. Often found in open country, farmland, and gardens. Bold and frequently perch-hunts from exposed wires and treetops.

children.name: Lanius vittatus
children.description: A small, colourful shrike of South Asia. Grey cap, black mask, chestnut back, and white wing patches. Inhabits dry open scrub and cultivation. Often seen perched on wires and fences, hunting insects with rapid sallies.

children.name: Lanius bucephalus
children.description: Endemic to East Asia — Japan, Korea, and eastern China. A robust, large-headed shrike with chestnut upperparts, grey underparts, and a strong bill. Inhabits open woodland, farmland, and suburban areas. Its calls are short, harsh notes often given in series.
